task: Write a 25-page PhD-level whitepaper for Bluewave

document_spec.title_pattern: The [Core Concept]: How [Pillar 1], [Pillar 2], and [Pillar 3] Converge to Redefine the $[TAM] [Market Name]
document_spec.subtitle: A Bluewave Technical & Strategic Whitepaper
document_spec.target_length: 7,500-8,500 words (~25 formatted pages with tables, code blocks, and diagrams)
document_spec.academic_level: PhD-level interdisciplinary paper spanning three domains
document_spec.tone: Authoritative, evidence-based, precise. Not promotional — analytical. Use data and citations to build arguments, not superlatives.
document_spec.citation_style: APA-style inline citations with full References section at the end. Minimum 12 peer-reviewed or canonical academic references.
document_spec.audience: VCs, enterprise buyers, strategic partners, and internal leadership. Readers who evaluate businesses through frameworks, not feature lists.

frameworks_to_apply: Porter's Five Forces (buyer power, supplier switching costs)
Al Ries & Jack Trout Positioning Theory (category creation vs. feature competition)
TAM/SAM/SOM recalculation through category reframing
Channel theory (agency as distribution channel, reseller arithmetic)
Wedge strategy (narrow entry point that reframes buyer perception)
Competitive positioning matrix (before/after transformation)
topics_to_cover: Market size and growth trajectory with data points
Content production crisis (supply/demand gap with statistics)
Category creation playbook (DAM tool → AI Creative Operations Agent)
Competitive positioning shift with comparison table
White-label economics and the agency multiplier effect
Distribution as competitive moat (last-mile thesis)
Revenue-ordered implementation roadmap

frameworks_to_apply: Wooldridge & Jennings agent properties (autonomy, social ability, reactivity, proactivity, learning)
OODA loop (Observe-Orient-Decide-Act) mapped to agent architecture
Protocol pattern for AI model evolution
Graceful degradation engineering
Multi-tenant AI isolation architecture
Autonomy ladder (Level 0-5 spectrum)
topics_to_cover: Vision pipeline: ingestion → multimodal analysis → graceful degradation
Compliance engine: guidelines → prompt construction → structured scoring (0-100)
Brand Voice learning: stateless vs. semi-stateful vs. fully stateful AI layers
AIServiceProtocol pattern with actual code snippets from the codebase
Multi-tenant data isolation (TenantMixin + session events)
AI isolation at the prompt level (contextual boundaries)
Usage metering architecture (millicent precision, token tracking)
Stub fallback mechanism for development/testing

frameworks_to_apply: Maslow's hierarchy adapted for organizational buyers (Kenrick et al., 2010)
Jobs-To-Be-Done (Christensen et al., 2016)
Loss aversion and endowment effect (Kahneman, Tversky)
Psychological reactance theory (Brehm, 1966)
Automation trust calibration (Lee & See, 2004)
Mere exposure effect (Zajonc, 1968)
Availability heuristic (Tversky & Kahneman, 1973)
Payment psychology / pain of paying (Prelec & Loewenstein, 1998)
topics_to_cover: Three-layer pain taxonomy (functional → identity → existential)
Approval bottleneck as organizational neurosis
Buyer segmentation by decision psychology (agency vs. mid-market vs. enterprise)
Temporal data moat as psychological lock-in (endowment effect, not reactance)
Five-layer switching cost model with temporal staircase
Why usage-based pricing resolves the B2B payment psychology paradox
Compliance-as-revenue: dual value proposition (cost reducer + risk reducer)
ROI proof loop: self-justifying software that prevents churn
Executive report as retention mechanism (mere exposure + availability heuristic)
Trust calibration through graduated transparency (scores, not binaries)

chapter_structure.title: Executive Summary
chapter_structure.instructions: Synthesize the entire thesis in one section. State the market size, the category shift (tool → agent), the economic impact (5-6x ARPU, 2x LTV), and the three converging forces. End with what the paper will examine. This should stand alone as a complete argument.

chapter_structure.title: The Market Inflection: Why Now
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections: (1) Content Production Crisis — use specific statistics (51% search time, 33% of marketers 3 weeks/year, $300B waste, 52% miss deadlines). (2) AI Capability Threshold — explain why pre-2024 AI was assistive and why multimodal vision crossed the threshold to agency. Use Bluewave's upload pipeline as the concrete example. (3) Market Size Recalculation — reframe TAM from $5.4B (DAM) to $15B+ (Creative Ops) by including adjacent markets.

chapter_structure.title: The Pain Taxonomy: A Psychographic Analysis
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections: (1) Three-layer pain model (functional/identity/existential) with concrete examples and the psychological escalation from 'we should probably get a DAM' to 'we need this fixed by Q2'. (2) Approval bottleneck as organizational neurosis — map creator/reviewer/manager anxieties and show how AI compliance scoring is a psychological intervention. (3) Buyer segmentation table with decision psychology (agency=external pressure, mid-market=internal accountability, enterprise=loss aversion).

chapter_structure.title: From Tool to Agent: The Architectural Thesis
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections: (1) Define agency using Wooldridge & Jennings' 4 properties + add 'learning' as 5th. Show how traditional DAMs satisfy none and Bluewave satisfies all. Use a comparison table. (2) Map agent loop to OODA framework with ASCII diagram. (3) Explain the Protocol pattern with actual Python code from ai_service.py. Emphasize that AI model evolution never requires route handler changes.

chapter_structure.title: The AI Engineering Stack: Vision, Compliance, and Voice
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections matching the three AI layers: (1) Vision Pipeline — 3 stages (ingestion, multimodal analysis, graceful degradation). Include the actual system prompts used. (2) Compliance Engine — describe the flow from guidelines to ComplianceResult. Include the Python dataclass code. Explain graduated routing (>=90 auto-submit, 70-89 warnings, <70 block). (3) Brand Voice Learning — explain stateless vs. semi-stateful vs. fully stateful. Describe the few-shot learning mechanism. State the moat thesis: 'This is the data moat.'

chapter_structure.title: The Data Moat: Temporal Lock-In
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections: (1) Moat theory in SaaS — enumerate the 4 classic moat types. Explain why traditional DAMs have weak moats. (2) The temporal data moat — timeline table showing capability and switching cost at months 1/3/6/12/18. (3) Psychological dimension — use endowment effect and loss aversion to explain why data moats are psychologically stronger than contractual lock-in. Include the 2.25x loss aversion coefficient.

chapter_structure.title: Behavioral Economics of Switching Costs
chapter_structure.instructions: Two subsections: (1) Five-layer switching cost model — Layer 1: data migration ($500-2K), Layer 2: workflow reconstruction ($5K-15K), Layer 3: brand intelligence loss (unquantifiable), Layer 4: team retraining ($3K-8K), Layer 5: relationship disruption (multiplicative for agencies). (2) The switching cost staircase — ASCII timeline showing accumulation from month 0 to 18+.

chapter_structure.title: Outcome-Based Pricing Psychology
chapter_structure.instructions: Four subsections: (1) Why per-seat pricing fails for AI (value is concentrated, variable, and demonstrable). (2) Hybrid model breakdown (seats + usage + modules) with specific price points. (3) Psychology of usage-based pricing — cite Prelec & Loewenstein on pain of paying. Explain why B2B resolves the paradox differently. Reference Intercom Fin ($0.99/resolution, 393% growth). (4) Metering architecture — describe AIUsageLog fields including cost_millicents precision.

chapter_structure.title: The Compliance Autonomy Spectrum
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections: (1) Autonomy ladder (Level 0-5) with clear definitions. Position competitors at L1, Bluewave current at L2-3, roadmap at L4-5. (2) Trust calibration — cite Lee & See's 3 failure modes (disuse, misuse, abuse). Explain how graduated scoring prevents all three. (3) Compliance-as-revenue — pricing arbitrage ($450+ Bynder vs. $29 Bluewave for same capability). 78% fewer brand incidents stat.

chapter_structure.title: Multi-Tenant AI Isolation
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections: (1) The isolation imperative — why trust requires absolute tenant separation. (2) Row-level isolation — TenantMixin code, session event listener. (3) AI isolation at prompt level — stateless model = architectural isolation by default. Key insight: learning is in the data curation, not model weights, so it persists across model upgrades.

chapter_structure.title: The Agency Multiplier
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections: (1) Reseller arithmetic — concrete numbers ($49/user + $29/portal → $1,025/month vs. $180 before = 5.7x). (2) Channel theory — Porter's buyer power analysis. Switching cost multiplicative effect (agency → clients → clients' users). (3) Portal as brand extension — transforms cost center to revenue enabler, eliminates price sensitivity.

chapter_structure.title: Distribution as Control
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections: (1) Workflow completion imperative — Brief→Create→Review→Approve→Publish→Analyze, DAMs only cover middle. 4.3 tools average for last mile. (2) Distribution as second data moat (performance learning on output side, complementing brand voice on input side). (3) Auto-resize intelligence — show how 1 upload → 8 AI actions → $0.40 usage revenue. 5x per-asset monetization.

chapter_structure.title: The ROI Proof Loop
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections: (1) Churn equation — introduce the third variable (increase visibility of existing value). (2) Self-justifying dashboard — 4 specific metrics with exact copy ('Bluewave saved your team an estimated $14,200 this month'). (3) Executive report as retention mechanism — solve the 'champion left the company' churn pattern. Cite Zajonc mere exposure effect and Tversky availability heuristic.

chapter_structure.title: Category Creation vs. Feature Competition
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections: (1) Category creation playbook — Ries & Trout positioning theory. Comparison table (category competition vs. creation). (2) Full positioning matrix (before/after on 8 dimensions). (3) Wedge strategy — the upload-to-caption pipeline as the narrow entry point. 'The category reframe happens in the user's mind, not in the marketing copy.'

chapter_structure.title: Implementation Architecture
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections: (1) Revenue-ordered roadmap table (Phase 1-17 with feature, revenue mechanism, cumulative multiplier). Cite Lean Startup principle. (2) Phase-gate criteria (technical/revenue/adoption/documentation checkpoints). (3) The 60/40 rule — 60% infrastructure done, 40% revenue-expansion remaining. '17 weeks to full platform.'

chapter_structure.title: Conclusion: The Agent Inevitability
chapter_structure.instructions: Three subsections: (1) Thesis restated — three converging forces. (2) Inevitability argument — economic pressure (8.6x cost advantage vs. hiring), AI capability trajectory, competitive cascade dynamics. (3) First-mover advantage in data moats — land-grab dynamic. End with: 'The agent is inevitable. The question is whose agent it will be.'

required_elements.tables: Minimum 10 tables throughout the document. Use tables for comparisons, timelines, frameworks, and data presentation.
required_elements.code_blocks: Minimum 3 actual code snippets from the Bluewave codebase (AIServiceProtocol, ComplianceResult dataclass, TenantMixin, _create_ai_service factory). Use Python syntax highlighting.
required_elements.ascii_diagrams: Minimum 2 ASCII art diagrams (OODA agent loop, switching cost staircase, or autonomy ladder).
required_elements.statistics: Every market claim must have a specific number and source attribution (e.g., '51% of employees — Wakefield Research, 2024').
required_elements.pricing_math: Include concrete revenue calculations with actual Bluewave price points.
required_elements.references_section: Full academic references in APA format. Minimum 12 citations from: Kahneman, Tversky, Porter, Christensen, Wooldridge & Jennings, Ries & Trout, Ries (Lean Startup), Brehm, Boyd, Lee & See, Zajonc, Prelec & Loewenstein, Greenwald & Kahn, Kenrick et al.

product_context.tagline: You upload. The agent does the rest.
product_context.one_liner: The AI agent that sees your media, writes your copy, enforces your brand, and ships your content — on autopilot.

market_data.missed_deadlines: 52% miss deadlines due to approval delays
market_data.compliance_improvement: 78% fewer brand incidents with AI-enhanced DAM

tech_stack.frontend: React 18 + TypeScript 5.5 + Vite 5.4 + Tailwind 3.4 + React Query

architecture_details.multi_tenancy: Row-level isolation via TenantMixin + SQLAlchemy session event listener auto-injects WHERE tenant_id = :tid
architecture_details.usage_metering: AIUsageLog table with tenant_id, user_id, asset_id, action_type, input_tokens, output_tokens, cost_millicents
architecture_details.compliance_scoring: 0-100 score with severity levels (error/warning/info), categories (color/logo/tone/font/hashtag), and actionable suggestions
architecture_details.brand_voice_learning: Few-shot learning from N most recent approved assets included in generation prompts

style_rules: Never use 'we believe' or 'we think' — use 'we argue', 'the data demonstrates', 'this analysis shows'
Every claim about the market must have a statistic or citation
Use 'This is not X. This is Y.' as a rhetorical device sparingly but effectively (max 3 times)
Tables should be used for any comparison with 3+ dimensions
Code blocks should be actual code from the codebase, not pseudocode
Each chapter must have exactly 2-3 subsections numbered X.1, X.2, X.3
The conclusion must end with a memorable one-liner that captures the thesis
No emojis, no bullet-heavy marketing copy, no feature lists disguised as analysis
Academic tone throughout — this should read like a Harvard Business Review article meets an ACM paper
Use 'temporal data moat' consistently as the proprietary term for Bluewave's competitive advantage

context_files_to_read.what_to_extract: tech stack, architecture, AI integration, execution phases, positioning
5 high-impact moves, pricing model, revenue projections, data moat explanation, competitive positioning
market size, pain points with statistics, competitor analysis, buyer segments, ROI metrics
AIServiceProtocol code, ClaudeAIService implementation, vision pipeline, fallback mechanism
ComplianceResult dataclass, scoring logic, issue categories
AIActionType enum, cost_millicents field, metering schema
guideline fields (colors, tone, dos/donts, custom rules)
TenantMixin, session event listener for isolation
current pricing tiers displayed to users
